title: Somatic Therapy for Racial Trauma: Healing in the Body, Healing in Community
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps heal racial trauma stored in the body and rebuilds strength through community connection.
body:
Understanding Racial Trauma Beyond Words

Racial trauma is not just a social or psychological phenomenon—it is deeply embodied. For many who experience racism, prejudice, microaggressions, and systemic oppression, the impact is felt in the nervous system and stored in the body’s physiology. As Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, has emphasized, trauma resides in the body, not just in memories or thoughts. This means traditional talk therapy often misses a crucial part of healing: addressing the somatic imprint of racial trauma.

Somatic therapy offers a pathway to access and regulate the nervous system, helping individuals process and release the physical tension and dysregulation caused by racial trauma. But healing racial trauma also requires more than individual work—it demands community and collective resilience-building. This article explores how somatic therapy engages both the body and community to foster healing and empowerment.

What Is Racial Trauma?

Racial trauma refers to the cumulative emotional and physical injury inflicted by experiences of racism and discrimination. Dr. Gabor Maté describes trauma as anything that overwhelms the nervous system’s ability to regulate itself, and racial trauma fits squarely within this definition. It includes overt acts of racism, chronic microaggressions, systemic inequities, and historical trauma passed down across generations.

Unlike a single traumatic event, racial trauma is often chronic and ongoing, which makes its effects more insidious. It can manifest as hypervigilance, anxiety, depression, somatic symptoms like chronic pain, and difficulty trusting others or feeling safe. This chronic stress dysregulates the autonomic nervous system, as Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains, reducing the body’s capacity to engage socially and regulate emotional states.

Physical Manifestations of Racial Trauma

  • Increased muscle tension and chronic pain
  • Digestive issues and headaches
  • Sleep disturbances and fatigue
  • Heightened startle response and hyperarousal
  • Disconnection from bodily sensations and emotions

Recognizing these symptoms as trauma responses rather than personal flaws is crucial for compassionate healing.

Why Somatic Therapy Works for Racial Trauma

Somatic therapy focuses on the body as the gateway to healing trauma. Peter Levine, creator of Somatic Experiencing, highlights how trauma is stored in the body’s autonomic nervous system and how mindful awareness of bodily sensations can unlock stuck energy and restore regulation. This approach is especially powerful for racial trauma because the body often holds the unprocessed stress of racialized experiences long after the conscious mind has moved on.

Somatic therapy incorporates techniques like breathwork, movement, grounding, and tracking bodily sensations to help clients reconnect with their bodies and nervous system. This helps shift the nervous system from a state of chronic fight, flight, or freeze into a regulated state where healing can occur. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy similarly integrates body awareness with cognitive and emotional processing, offering tools to renegotiate traumatic experiences stored somatically.

Importantly, somatic therapy validates the lived experience of racial trauma by honoring the body’s intelligence and its role in survival. It creates a safe space where clients can begin to restore a sense of safety and reclaim agency over their bodies.

Community as a Crucial Healing Context

Healing racial trauma cannot happen in isolation. Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal Theory underscores the importance of social engagement systems in nervous system regulation. Feeling connected and seen by others activates the ventral vagal pathway, promoting safety and co-regulation.

For people impacted by racial trauma, community offers a vital container where shared experiences validate pain and reduce isolation. Collective healing practices—whether group somatic therapy, support circles, or culturally informed rituals—can foster resilience and empowerment. The work of Richard Schwartz, founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), also illustrates how community can help individuals witness and hold parts of themselves that have been wounded by racialized trauma.

“Healing racial trauma means healing the body’s nervous system and rebuilding trust through community connection.”

Examples of Community-Based Somatic Healing

  • Somatic support groups: Groups that combine somatic techniques with shared storytelling and validation
  • Movement and breath workshops: Culturally grounded practices that reconnect participants to their bodies
  • Healing circles: Spaces that honor ancestral trauma and promote collective resilience
  • Community rituals: Ceremonies that embody collective mourning and renewal

Practical Somatic Tools for Self-Regulation

While professional guidance is key, individuals can cultivate nervous system regulation through simple somatic tools. Dan Siegel’s concept of "mindsight"—the ability to observe one’s internal experience with curiosity—can be harnessed through these practices:

  • Body scanning: Systematically noticing sensations from head to toe to build awareness and presence
  • Grounding techniques: Pressing feet into the floor or placing hands on the heart to anchor oneself
  • Breath awareness: Slow, deep breathing to activate the parasympathetic nervous system
  • Movement breaks: Gentle stretching or shaking to discharge tension

These tools help interrupt the chronic stress cycle by shifting the nervous system toward safety. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy also integrates somatic awareness with eye movement to process trauma, highlighting the synergy between different modalities.

The Intersection of Somatic Healing and Social Justice

Healing racial trauma through somatic therapy is not just a personal journey—it intersects with systemic change and social justice work. Gabor Maté points out that trauma is rooted in societal conditions, so healing must include addressing social determinants of health and structural inequalities.

Practitioners and communities engaging in somatic healing can become advocates for dismantling oppression by embodying resilience and fostering spaces where marginalized voices are honored. This embodied activism creates ripples beyond the individual, contributing to collective liberation.

Finding the Right Somatic Therapist for Racial Trauma

Not all somatic therapists are trained or culturally competent to work with racial trauma. It is crucial to find practitioners who:

  • Understand the systemic and historical context of racial trauma
  • Are trained in somatic modalities such as Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed therapy
  • Center racial justice and cultural humility in their practice
  • Create a safe, validating environment for exploring trauma

Resources such as the Somatic Experiencing Trauma Institute and professional directories often list culturally competent providers.

Conclusion: Healing in the Body and the Collective

Racial trauma imprints itself deeply in the body’s nervous system, often beyond what words can reach. Somatic therapy offers a science-backed approach to accessing, regulating, and releasing this trauma, facilitating profound healing. However, because racial trauma is rooted in systemic oppression and collective histories, healing requires connection, community, and social engagement.

By combining somatic tools with community-based practices, individuals can reclaim their bodies, restore nervous system regulation, and build resilience that reverberates through families and communities. As we heal in our bodies and with each other, we also heal the social fabric torn by racism.

For those who feel stuck after talk therapy or traditional approaches, somatic therapy offers a grounded, embodied path forward—a way to listen to the body’s wisdom and reclaim safety both inside and out.

References:

  • van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. North Atlantic Books.
  • Siegel, D. (2010). The Mindful Therapist. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: What is racial trauma and how does it differ from other types of trauma?
faqs.a: Racial trauma is the cumulative impact of racism and discrimination on an individual's nervous system. Unlike singular traumatic events, it is often chronic and systemic, affecting body and mind over time.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with racial trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy works by addressing trauma stored in the body’s nervous system through techniques like breathwork, movement, and body awareness, helping to regulate and release traumatic stress.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic healing on my own at home?
faqs.a: Yes, simple somatic practices like grounding, breath awareness, and body scanning can be done independently to support nervous system regulation, but working with a trained somatic therapist is recommended for deeper trauma work.

faqs.q: Why is community important in healing racial trauma?
faqs.a: Community provides safety, validation, and social engagement that activate the body’s parasympathetic system, essential for healing. Collective spaces honor shared experiences and foster resilience.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and introduces somatic approaches to healing.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Offers a detailed exploration of Somatic Experiencing and how the body processes trauma.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Explains how understanding the vagus nerve and social engagement can aid trauma recovery.
